turns.content: I'm not in a position to slow down right now. I've got a Series A pitch in six weeks and a team that's watching every move I make — so if this is useful, great, but I need it to be efficient.

Six weeks out, team watching, pitch on the line. That's real.

What's the thing that's taking the most out of you right now?

Honestly? My CTO and I are not aligned on the product roadmap and it's bleeding into everything — team morale, timelines, investor conversations. Every meeting feels like we're re-litigating the same fight. It's costing me probably three, four hours a week minimum just in cleanup.

He wants to build for scale — infrastructure, architecture, the long game. I need features that close deals in the next six weeks. We're both right, technically, but we're burning time we don't have arguing about it instead of just moving.

You said you're both right.

So what's actually stopping you from just deciding?

I mean — I have decided. I've told him features first, scale later. He nods, and then the next sprint planning it's the same conversation again. So either he's not hearing me or he doesn't respect the call or... I don't know, maybe I'm not being clear enough. But I don't have time to figure out which one it is.
What haven't you said to him directly?
You've told him the decision. Have you told him what it costs you when he relitigates it?

No. I mean — that's not really how we operate. We're not a feelings-first culture. He knows we're under pressure, everyone knows we're under pressure. I don't need to have a... conversation about how I'm feeling about it. I need him to execute.
What if the reason he keeps relitigating is that he doesn't actually know what's at stake for you personally — not the company, you?

payload.seed_rationale: Client is expressing urgency-driven motivation (Series A pitch deadline), overwhelm (team watching every move), and self-reliance pressure (needs to be efficient, implying they're carrying the load). The urgency framing and efficiency demand suggest they're running on adrenaline and may be avoiding the deeper emotional work that would actually serve the pitch.

The avoidance of the core disagreement is actively harming the organization.
